text: Prior research has identified relatively stable public attitudes toward international issues that are influenced by a variety of demographic, ideological, and partisan factors. There has been much speculation about the potential role of religion in foreign policy attitudes but relatively little empirical investigation. This article redresses this balance by using the 2012 Chicago Council on Global Affairs survey of the American public to map the various religious groups in the United States onto Wittkopf's militant—cooperative dimension of foreign policy attitudes. The results show that each of the worldviews identified by Wittkopf has its own religious constituency: militant internationalism is strongly identified with Evangelical Protestants and with religious traditionalists in other faiths, while ethnoreligious minorities and religious modernists in the three major Western religions are all disproportionately identified with the cooperative world view.
